title: Chat Needs a Canvas
description: Part 2 of a 6-part series on AI-native computing. Conversation is the wrong shape for creation — the fix is not going back to apps, but letting tools return interfaces, not just text. Companion video to the essay of the same name.

beats.text: Chat needs a canvas. This is part two of a six part series about where computing goes next. Part one asked what replaces the app. This part asks a smaller, sharper question. When a tool answers you — why should it only answer in text?

eyebrow.label: Part 2 of 6 · Beyond the Sea of App Icons

beats.text: Elena is sixty-eight, and her left knee has been bad since March. At the clinic, there's no clipboard. Just a conversation. What brings you in today? The knee again, she says. Worse on stairs. And then a form appears — right there, in the chat.

image.html:
A form, authored mid-conversation
🩺
Let's get you checked in, Elena.
Patient intake
Name
Elena M.
Date of birth
MM / DD / YYYY
Reason for visit
Knee pain — left▾
Current medications
Warfarin 5 mg daily
First visit?YesNo
Submit
The knee again — worse on stairs.

beats.text: Five fields, not forty. Which knee — already filled in. Pain on stairs, scored on its own, because she mentioned stairs. One line to confirm her blood thinner dose. Nothing her record already knows. Ninety seconds, start to finish. And here's the strange part. Nobody designed this form. It did not exist until she said the word knee.

slide.title: Five fields, not forty

items.title: Blood thinner
items.description: confirm the one dose that matters

items.title: Imaging elsewhere?
items.description: one checkbox — nothing her record knows

slide.footer: Nobody designed it. It did not exist until she said “knee.”

beats.text: Why does that matter? Because chat is a scroll. It only grows downward. Superb for questions — terrible for creation. A designer needs a canvas. An analyst needs a table she can sort. A patient needs five labeled fields, not five questions asked one at a time. A transcript can describe a spreadsheet perfectly. You still can't drag a cell.

eyebrow.label: A scroll is not a workshop

left.title: Chat is a scroll

items: You can't drag a cell in a transcript

left.footer: Describing a spreadsheet isn't a spreadsheet

right.title: Creation needs surfaces

items: An analyst needs a sortable table

right.footer: The fix isn't going back to apps

callout.text: Keep the conversation as the spine — and let tools return {primary:interfaces}.
callout.label: The fix

beats.text: The mechanism is small — and that's the point. Every model already knows how to call a tool. The change is this: the tool answers with two payloads. Words for the model. Form created, five fields — that's all it ever sees. And an interface for you. A real form, rendered live, beside the conversation. When Elena taps submit, her answers flow back in as data. One loop. The interface never leaves the dialogue.

content.value: That's all the model ever sees — nothing changes on its side.
**An interface.** A form, a chart, a map — rendered live beside the transcript.

content.text: Submit → your answers rejoin the conversation as data.

beats.text: And it's not just forms. Ask for the present value of a thousand dollars a month — and make the discount rate easy to change. A live spreadsheet opens beside the chat. Drag the rate, and the table recomputes while you keep talking. Claude's Artifacts and ChatGPT's Canvas are the same instinct: a live surface, opening next to the words.

image.html:
The tool returned an interface, not a paragraph
Show me the present value of $1,000 a month for a year — let me change the discount rate.
✨
Here you go — drag the rate and the table recomputes.
Present value of $1,000 / month
Discount rate4.0%
MonthPaymentPresent value
1$1,000.00$996.68
2$1,000.00$993.37
3$1,000.00$990.07
⋯
12$1,000.00$960.85
Total$12,000.00$11,744.16

beats.text: Here's what tells you the idea is real: everyone arrived at once. Vercel shipped generative U I in early twenty twenty-four. M C P dash U I pioneered it over an open protocol in May twenty twenty-five. That October, OpenAI launched its Apps S D K. And in November, Anthropic and OpenAI — direct competitors — cosigned one standard, M C P Apps. When rivals co-author the substrate, it has stopped being anyone's thesis.

slide.title: Four roads to the same shape
slide.subtitle: Convergence is validation, not priority

items.title: Vercel AI SDK 3.0
items.description: “Generative UI” — tool calls stream components into chat

items.title: MCP-UI
items.description: The pioneer over an open protocol — adopted by Shopify, Postman

items.title: OpenAI Apps SDK
items.description: Full apps inside ChatGPT, built on MCP

items.title: MCP Apps (SEP-1865)
items.description: Cosigned by Anthropic + OpenAI — rivals co-author the substrate

beats.text: So the interesting question moved. M C P Apps is well designed. A developer ships the interface as a fixed template, and the host can audit it before it ever runs. That fixedness is the security model — it's deliberate. But run Elena through it. The developer designs one good intake form. The model can summon it. It can never reshape it. Not for her knee. Not for her blood thinner.

slide.title: Summon a form — or write one?

left.title: The app's form

items: A template, fixed at development time
Auditable before it runs — that's the trust model
The model can summon it, never shape it

left.footer: One good form, for every patient

right.title: The model's form

items: Fields chosen for this patient, this visit
The blood-thinner line, because it matters

right.footer: Authored at runtime, in the conversation

callout.text: Both camps agree tools should return interfaces. They differ on {highlight:authorship.}
callout.label: The real disagreement

beats.text: The disagreement is smaller and sharper than competing protocols. It's about who holds the pen. In M C P Apps, the app holds the pen. Here, the model does. And that's the first appearance of the second pillar — the computer that builds — showing up at the interface, before it ever touches logic.

slide.quote: In MCP Apps, the {info:app} holds the pen; here, the {primary:model} does.
slide.author: Chat Needs a Canvas

beats.text: Remember the feature ratchet from part one? Graphing, rebuilt in the spreadsheet, the tracker, the C R M — and paid for every single time. Here's the fix, as a working mechanism. Present form. Present chart. Present markdown. One shared toolkit, owned by the conversation — and the model composes it across every domain. No Mac app ships its own scroll bar. No tool here ships its own chart engine.

slide.title: One toolkit, every domain
slide.footer: Written once. Composed by the model. Every application slims down.

beats.text: Now look at what's left of the receptionist that greeted Elena. A list of tool names. And a paragraph telling the model how a receptionist behaves. That's the whole application. No custom code. No custom screens. Swap the list and the paragraph, and the same shell becomes a recipe guide — or a trip planner. The role is the application.

items: A tool list — presentForm, records lookup…
A system prompt — “act as a receptionist”

content.text: No custom code. No custom screens.

right.title: Swap it, get a new app

content.value: Same shell — only the list and the paragraph change.

beats.text: Two honest problems. If vendors ship branded widgets into chat, the sea of icons doesn't die — it migrates into the conversation. An app store, one level down. And interfaces generated per person are interfaces no two people share. Documentation, support, a granddaughter showing Elena where to tap — all of that assumes a common surface. Part of the answer is constraint. Part of it has no answer yet.

eyebrow.label: The part I cannot wave away

slide.title: Two honest problems

items.title: The icon sea migrates
items.description: Branded widgets competing for placement in the transcript are an app store inside a chat window.

items.title: No two screens alike
items.description: Generated UI breaks the shared surface — documentation, support, showing someone where to tap.

slide.footer: Interactive UI in chat is necessary — and sufficient for none of this.

beats.text: This was part two of six — and the short version. The full essay has the whole record: who arrived when, what M C P Apps gets right, and the costs, stated in full. Next, part three. If the model writes the interface — and someday the logic — what makes its output trustworthy? The answer is a language that gives up power on purpose.

slide.title: Chat Needs a **Canvas**
slide.subtitle: Part 3: {primary:DSLs as Harnesses} — a language that gives up power on purpose
chips: 1 · Beyond the sea of app icons
